examples: This was their fourth successive win.
Successive governments have tried to tackle the problem.
There has been low rainfall for two successive years.
Who's the likely successor to him as party leader?
Their latest release is a worthy successor to their popular debut album.
He chose as his successor a relative newcomer to the organization.
to suck the juice from an orange
She was noisily sucking up milk through a straw.
He sucked the blood from a cut on his finger.
They threatened to sue if the work was not completed.
sue (somebody/something) for something to sue somebody for breach of contract
to sue somebody for $10 million (= in order to get money from somebody)
to sue somebody for damages
He threatened to sue the company for negligence.
sue somebody/something The water authority was successfully sued over his illness.
to commit suicide
an attempted suicide (= one in which the person survives)
a suicide bomber (= who expects to die while trying to kill other people with a bomb)
a hotel/private/honeymoon suite
a suite of rooms/offices
We reached the summit at noon.
This path leads to the summit.
(figurative) the summit of his career
a superb player
The car's in superb condition.
His performance was absolutely superb.
You look superb.
vastly superior
superior intelligence
Liverpool were clearly the superior team.
superior to somebody/something This model is technically superior to its competitors.
She felt socially superior to most of her neighbours.
The enemy won because of their superior numbers (= there were more of them).
supervise (somebody/something) to supervise building work
Who is supervising?
supervise somebody doing something She supervised the children playing near the pool.
Very young children should not be left to play without supervision.
under supervision The drug should only be used under medical supervision.
I have a meeting with my supervisor about my research topic.
All work is done under the guidance of a supervisor.
vitamin/dietary supplements (= vitamins and other foods eaten in addition to what you usually eat)
supplement to something Industrial sponsorship is a supplement to government funding.
supplement something with something a diet supplemented with vitamin pills
supplement something He supplements his income by giving private lessons.
a supportive family
She was very supportive during my father's illness.
a mutually supportive relationship
He was strongly supportive of my career.
The novel is supposedly based on a true story.
a poem supposedly written by Shakespeare
Police have found the supposedly stolen car.
The rebellion was brutally suppressed.
the Supreme Commander of the armed forces
the supreme champion
It is an event in which she reigns supreme.
She felt a sudden surge of anger.
a surge of excitement
The gates opened and the crowd surged forward.
Flood waters surged into their homes.
He surged past the other runners on the last lap.
surgical procedures
a surgical ward (= for patients having operations)
food surpluses
in surplus Wheat was in surplus that year.
The rebel soldiers were forced to surrender.
surrender to somebody He surrendered voluntarily to his enemies.
surrender yourself (to somebody) The hijackers eventually surrendered themselves to the police.
The police are keeping the suspects under constant surveillance.
surveillance cameras/equipment
suspension from school
The two players are appealing against their suspensions.
They drove away slowly to avoid arousing suspicion.
on suspicion of something He was arrested on suspicion of murder.
suspicion that… I have a sneaking suspicion that she's not telling the truth.
My suspicions were confirmed when police raided the property.
She was reluctant to voice her suspicions.
His resignation seemed only to fuel suspicions.
It’s time to confront him with our suspicions.
They became suspicious of his behaviour and contacted the police.
a suspicious look
You have a very suspicious mind (= you always think that people are behaving in an illegal or dishonest way).
Which planets can sustain life?
I only had a little chocolate to sustain me on my walk.
The love and support of his family sustained him during his time in prison.
When she lost her job they could no longer sustain their expensive lifestyle.
The soil was so badly eroded it could no longer sustain crop production.
He took a wild swing at the ball.
the swing of her hips
His arms swung as he walked.
As he pushed her, she swung higher and higher (= while sitting on a swing).
swing from something A set of keys swung from her belt.
swing something He sat on the stool, swinging his legs.
to draw/sheathe a sword (= to take it out of/put it into its cover)
He shook his fist in a symbolic gesture of defiance.
The ceremony has a deep symbolic meaning.
The new regulations are largely symbolic (= they will not have any real effect).
symbolic of something The dove is symbolic of peace.
PMS or premenstrual syndrome
This syndrome is associated with frequent coughing.
This syndrome mostly affects women in their forties.
synthesis of A with B the synthesis of art with everyday life
synthesis of A and B a synthesis of traditional and modern values
a systematic approach to solving the problem
a systematic attempt to destroy the organization
The prisoner was subjected to systematic torture.
the systematic analysis of the risks involved
He was booked for a late tackle on Torres.
He brought the burglar down with a flying tackle.
They tried all kinds of tactics to get us to go.
This was just the latest in a series of delaying tactics.
The manager discussed tactics with his team.
Confrontation is not always the best tactic.
It's time to try a change of tactic.
They used strong-arm (= aggressive or violent) tactics.
tactical planning
to have a tactical advantage
Telling your boss you were looking for a new job was a tactical error (= it was the wrong thing to do at that time).
Hundreds of thousands of pounds of taxpayers' money (= money paid in taxes) have been spent on the project.
The proposals will be popular with higher-rate taxpayers.
tempt somebody I was tempted by the dessert menu.
Don't tempt thieves by leaving valuables clearly visible.
tempt somebody into (doing) something He was tempted into a life of crime.
tempt somebody to do something I was tempted to take the day off.
They had evicted their tenants for non-payment of rent.
The decorating was done by a previous tenant.
tenant farmers (= ones who do not own their own farms)
tender words
What he needs now is a lot of tender loving care (= sympathetic treatment).
his four-year tenure as president
She had a long tenure of office.
He has terminal lung cancer.
The illness is usually terminal.
(figurative) She's suffering from terminal (= very great) boredom.
Your contract of employment terminates in December.
terminate something The agreement was terminated immediately.
to terminate a pregnancy (= to perform or have an abortion)
difficult/rough/mountainous terrain
They walked for miles across steep and inhospitable terrain.
I feel absolutely terrific today!
She's doing a terrific job.
You look terrific in that dress.
He was really terrific when I was in trouble.
That’s terrific news!
(ironic) So nobody’s coming. Terrific!
testify against somebody/something She refused to testify against her husband.
testify for somebody/something There are several witnesses who will testify for the defence.
testify about something He was summoned to testify before a grand jury about his role in the affair.
testify to something/to doing something Evans testified to receiving $200 000 in bribes.
testify (that)… He testified (that) he was at the theatre at the time of the murder.
+ speech ‘I was approached by a man I did not recognize,’ she testified.
This increase in exports bears testimony to the successes of industry.
The pyramids are an eloquent testimony to the ancient Egyptians' engineering skills.
His thick, swollen fingers bore testimony to a lifetime of toil.
the soft texture of velvet
She uses a variety of different colours and textures in her wall hangings.
The method used will vary with the soil texture and climate.
There was a fire in the building, but thankfully no one was hurt.
Thankfully, the house was empty when I got home.
a theatrical agent
a degree in theology
a theology student
the rarefied world of academic theology
a theoretical approach
theoretical physics
The first year provides students with a sound theoretical basis for later study.
She married at 17 and gave birth to her first child shortly thereafter.
Regular exercise strengthens the heart, thereby reducing the risk of heart attack.
a brilliant and thought-provoking play
He looked thoughtful.
They sat in thoughtful silence.
a needle and thread
a robe embroidered with gold thread
the delicate threads of a spider’s web
You've pulled a thread in your jumper.
He stepped across the threshold.
on the threshold She stood hesitating on the threshold.
‘Are you pleased?’ ‘I'm thrilled.’
thrilled about/at something He was thrilled at the prospect of seeing them again.
thrilled with something I'm really thrilled with the results.
thrilled to do something I was thrilled to be invited.
thrilled that… (British English) She was thrilled to bits (= extremely pleased) that he'd been offered the job.
New businesses thrive in this area.
These animals rarely thrive in captivity.
the ebb and flow of the tide
The tide is in/out.
Is the tide coming in or going out?
The body was washed up on the beach by the tide.
tighten something to tighten a lid/screw/rope/knot
She tightened her grip on his arm.
The nuts weren't properly tightened and the wheel came off.
tighten something up The brake cable needs tightening up.
(+ adv./prep.) Her grip seemed to tighten on the door handle.
He saw his father's jaw tighten in irritation.
the timber industry
A nasty incident was prevented by the timely arrival of the police.
This has been a timely reminder to us all.
I was grateful for your timely intervention.
All claims must be dealt with in a professional and timely manner.
The timely intervention of a teacher meant a fight was avoided.
tobacco smoke
The government imposed a ban on tobacco advertising (= the advertising of cigarettes and all other forms of tobacco).
Several different tobaccos are grown on the land.
She had no tolerance for jokes of any kind.
religious tolerance
a reputation for tolerance of alternative lifestyles
tolerate something Their relationship was tolerated but not encouraged.
This sort of behaviour will not be tolerated.
tolerate (somebody/something) doing/being/having something She refused to tolerate being called a liar.
motorway tolls
a toll bridge
the possibility of imposing tolls on some motorways
Worldwide sales look set to top $1 billion.
Many of the refugees have suffered torture.
the use of torture
terrible instruments of torture
under torture His confessions were made under torture.
I heard stories of gruesome tortures in prisons.
torture somebody Many of the rebels were captured and tortured by secret police.
The prisoners were routinely tortured.
torture somebody into doing something He was tortured into giving them the information.
toss something + adv./prep. I tossed the book aside and got up.
toss something to somebody He tossed the ball to Anna.
toss somebody something He tossed Anna the ball.
Imports totalled $1.5 billion last year.
In 2005–6, college enrolments totalled some 5 400.
toxic chemicals/fumes/gases/substances
to dispose of toxic waste
Many pesticides are highly toxic.
toxic to somebody/something This chemical is toxic to many forms of life.
It's exciting to discover traces of earlier civilizations.
Police searched the area but found no trace of the escaped prisoners.
Years of living in England had eliminated all trace of her American accent.
without (a) trace The ship had vanished without (a) trace.
‘Big Mac’ is McDonald's best-known trademark.
a trail of blood
tourists who leave a trail of litter everywhere they go
The hurricane left a trail of destruction behind it.
trail something A jeep trailing a cloud of dust was speeding in my direction.
I trailed my hand in the water as the boat moved along.
(+ adv./prep.) The bride's dress trailed behind her.
a car towing a trailer with a boat on it
financial transactions between companies
commercial transactions
a transcript of the interview
The White House posted a transcript of the speech on its website.
The way in which we work has undergone a complete transformation in the past decade.
What a transformation! You look great.
transformation (from something) (to/into something) the country’s transformation from dictatorship to democracy
The cost includes transit.
in transit goods damaged in transit
transit times
the transmission of the disease
the risk of transmission
a need for greater transparency in legal documents
The police reforms will ensure greater transparency and accountability.
The insect's wings are almost transparent.
the effects of trauma and stress on the body
The children showed no signs of trauma after their ordeal.
patients who have histories of childhood trauma
the Treaty of Rome
a peace treaty
to draw up/sign/ratify a treaty
Under the terms of the treaty, La Rochelle was ceded to the English.
a tremendous explosion
A tremendous amount of work has gone into the project.
tribal art
tribal leaders
an international war crimes tribunal
a military tribunal
At her funeral her oldest friend paid tribute to her life and work.
The crowd stood in silent tribute to those who had died in the war.
This book is a fitting tribute to the bravery of the pioneers.
floral tributes (= gifts of flowers at a funeral)
to pull/squeeze the trigger
He kept his finger on the trigger.
A trio of English runners featured in the women’s 1 500 metres.
one of the greatest triumphs of modern science
triumph over somebody/something It was a personal triumph over her old rival.
a trophy cabinet
She looked into his troubled face.
She still felt vaguely troubled by it all.
The bank will act as trustees for the estate until the child is 18.
